cards.text: The {{c1::constexpr}} keyword defines a variable that must be a compile-time constant.
Unlike const, {{c1::constexpr}} guarantees the value is known at {{c2::compile-time}}.
To declare a constexpr variable: {{c1::constexpr int x{ 5 };}}
Best practice: Use {{c1::constexpr}} for compile-time constants, and {{c2::const}} for runtime constants.

cards.question: What's wrong with this code?
cards.answer: Compiler error: constexpr variables must be initialized with a constant expression. x is not known at compile-time.
cards.explanation: constexpr requires the value to be computable during compilation, but x depends on user input which is only available at runtime.

cards.question: Which declarations are valid?
cards.answer:
Valid: a, b, c
Invalid: e (d is not const or constexpr)
cards.explanation: constexpr variables can be initialized from other const/constexpr values that are compile-time constants, but not from non-const variables.

cards.front: What is the main difference between const and constexpr?
cards.back:
• const: value cannot change, but may be determined at runtime
  • constexpr: value must be known at compile-time and cannot change

cards.front: When should you use constexpr instead of const?
cards.back:
Use constexpr when:
  • The value is known at compile-time
  • You need a compile-time constant (array size, template argument)
  • You want to ensure it's evaluated at compile-time for optimization

cards.explanation:
constexpr provides both performance and correctness benefits:
  • Performance: Guarantees compile-time evaluation
  • Safety: Compiler enforces compile-time computability
  • Intent: Clearly documents that value is compile-time constant
The compiler will error if you accidentally try to use a runtime value.
examples:
constexpr int max{ 100 };
int arr[max];  // OK

constexpr double pi{ 3.14159 };
constexpr double area{ pi * r * r };  // Computed at compile-time

cards.back: Keyword that declares a variable or function that must be evaluable at compile-time
